reachability_fact:
THREE live defects confirmed by reading every line of each file:

(a) GROEBNER error-type propagation — groebner.iii:1041 (`gb_buch_step`): `if s == GROEBNER_SENT { return GROEBNER_E_INV }`. `gb_spoly` returns `GROEBNER_SENT` for five distinct reasons: NOSESS (line 759), zero-poly (lines 759-760), gfp_inv==0 (lines 791/793 — true E_INV), gb_mul_term full (lines 796/799 — E_FULL), gb_sub_new full (line 803 — E_FULL). The caller conflates all five as E_INV. The caller `gb_buchberger` (lines 1014-1019) then sets `rc = GROEBNER_E_INV` instead of `E_FULL`, causing `gb_buchberger` to return the wrong error code to the caller, making E_FULL appear as E_INV (a non-Groebner result signalled as a non-invertible field element). No existing `GROEBNER_SPOLY_STATUS` module var exists.

(a-typo) KAT typo — groebner.iii:1253 `return 491u64` and :1256 `return 511u64`. The KAT sequence uses sequential unique error codes (48, 49, 50, 51 at lines 1251-1255). Lines 1253 and 1256 are sub-checks within the same logical assertion pair; the codes should be unique integers continuing the sequence (492 and 512). `491` and `511` look like `49`+`1` and `51`+`1` with a decimal separator missing. They are non-colliding with 99 so they produce correct FAIL signals, but they break the monotone sequential code convention and are visually confusing.

(b) HDL shift defect — hdl_optimize.iii:43 `let rows : u64 = 1u64 << nn` and :56 `let mask : u64 = 1u64 << r`. Compiled to `shlq %cl, %rax` (assembly line 74 and 199 in numera_hdl_optimize.iii.o.s). On x86-64, `shlq %cl, %rax` masks the shift count to 6 bits (cl & 63). So for n=7 (rows=128), `r` reaches 64, and `1u64 << 64` executes as `1u64 << 0 = 1` — the truth table re-sets bit 0, producing a WRONG function fingerprint for n>=7. Also `1u64 << 64` for rows computation gives 1 not 128, cutting the iteration short. All callers in scope use n=2 or n=3 (verified by grep), so the KAT passes — the defect is latent but REAL and reachable for any caller passing n>=7. No guard exists: `HG_MAXIN=64` allows up to 64 inputs.

(c) HKDF HMAC-OOM propagation — hkdf.iii:53 `hmac_sha256(k, k_len, ikm, ikm_len)` — return value discarded. hkdf.iii:54 `hmac_sha256_tag((&HKDF_WORK_PRK) as u64 as *u8)` — return value discarded. hkdf.iii:89 `hmac_sha256(pp, 32u64, mp, mi)` — return value discarded. hkdf.iii:90 `hmac_sha256_tag(tp)` — return value discarded. `hmac_sha256` (hmac.iii:124) calls `hmac_alloc_buffers()` which calls `arena_new(4096)` + 3× `arena_alloc1`; `arena_alloc1` is proven to return 0u64 on OOM (region.iii:139-140). If HMAC init fails, `hmac_sha256` returns `HMAC_E_INIT = -1i32`. HKDF discards this, sets `HKDF_PRK_READY=1`, and the caller gets a garbage PRK with exit code `HKDF_OK`. The inner SHA-256 was never finalized, so `hmac_sha256_tag` extracts leftover hash state. The RFC 5869 KAT passes only because the 4096-byte arena is sufficient for 320 bytes — but the failure path is completely unguarded.
maximal_design:
(a) GROEBNER error-type propagation + KAT typo — maximal fix:

Add `GROEBNER_SPOLY_STATUS : i32` module variable (matching the existing `GROEBNER_RS_STATUS` pattern). In `gb_spoly`, before every `return GROEBNER_SENT`, set this variable to the appropriate error code: GROEBNER_E_NOSESS, GROEBNER_E_BAD, GROEBNER_E_INV, or GROEBNER_E_FULL. On success set it to GROEBNER_OK. In `gb_buch_step`, after `gb_spoly` returns SENT, read `GROEBNER_SPOLY_STATUS` and return that code instead of blindly returning `GROEBNER_E_INV`. This is strictly better: error codes are now semantically correct, the Buchberger caller gets E_FULL vs E_INV faithfully, and callers can distinguish storage-overflow from field-invertibility.

KAT typo: change `491u64` to `492u64` and `511u64` to `512u64` — these are the natural next sequential sub-codes after 49 and 51.

(b) HDL shift defect — maximal fix:

Add an `n <= 6u32` guard at the top of `hdl_opt_truth_table`. A u64 truth table covers at most 2^6=64 input assignments; n=7 requires 128 bits (does not fit). Return `0u64` immediately for n>6 — `0u64` is the falsey truth table (all outputs zero) which is a safe sentinel: no caller should treat a zero truth table as equivalent to a nonzero one. Also document the API bound in the function comment. This is strictly better: prevents the silent x86 shift-mask wrapping that would produce a wrong function fingerprint, closes the API surface to values that are provably representable.

(c) HKDF HMAC-OOM — maximal fix:

Add `const HKDF_E_HMAC : i32 = -3i32`. In `hkdf_sha256_extract`, bind the `hmac_sha256` return to a local, check against HKDF_OK, propagate failure (do NOT set HKDF_PRK_READY). Bind the `hmac_sha256_tag` return, check and propagate. In `hkdf_sha256_expand`, inside the loop, bind `hmac_sha256` return, check and break/return on failure; bind `hmac_sha256_tag` return and propagate. This is strictly better: a HMAC failure is faithfully surfaced to the HKDF caller, no garbage key material is silently accepted as a valid PRK/OKM, and the state flag HKDF_PRK_READY is only set when the extract step actually succeeded.

code_changes.new_code:
var GROEBNER_RS_STATUS   : i32 = 0i32
var GROEBNER_SPOLY_STATUS : i32 = 0i32

code_changes.anchor: fn gb_spoly(f: u32, g: u32) -> u32 @export {

    if s == GROEBNER_SENT { return GROEBNER_E_INV }
code_changes.new_code:
let s : u32 = gb_spoly(fpoly, gpoly)
    if s == GROEBNER_SENT { return GROEBNER_SPOLY_STATUS }
